title: Podman container in pod doesn't include `BindsTo` and `After=pod..` in systemd generated file
body:
### Issue Description

When you run pod with container inside and generate systemd files, it differs when generating from pod and from container.
When generating with `podman generate systemd <pod_name>` it generates systemd files both for pod and container, and container file includes lines:
```ini
BindsTo=pod-53e4b6d3cdc3d2792713bb5aa077142069f2e71ecf8a79654ccb531e114a8149.service
After=pod-53e4b6d3cdc3d2792713bb5aa077142069f2e71ecf8a79654ccb531e114a8149.service
```
When generating with `podman generate systemd <container_name>` the container file does NOT include those lines. It creates an inconsistency. If container knows which pod it belongs to, it shouldn't be different results.

Discovered in scope of issue in Ansible Podman collection: https://github.com/containers/ansible-podman-collections/issues/645

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. `podman pod create --name testpod`
2. `podman run -dit --name testcontainer --pod testpod alpine sleep 1h`
3. `podman generate systemd testpod`
4. `podman generate systemd testcontainer`


### Describe the results you received

I've got different results for container systemd file when generating from pod and from container

### Describe the results you expected

Same systemd files

comments.body:
> @vrothberg Is this addressed in Quadlet?

Quadlet supports Pods via Kuberenetes YAML.

I am personally reluctant: the issue can be looked at in many ways.  Some may just want to generate a unit for this container, some for the entire pod.

Since this would be (somehow) a new feature, I would rather recommend using Quadlet as podman-generate-systemd is now deprecated and will only receive security or urgent bug fixes.

But I will leave the issue open for a while so we can take the time to discuss it.

If you generate the units for a pod you should runt he command on the pod and never on individual containers. 

Just practically speaking it is completely impossible to do this because we cannot know the pod unit name. The unit name can be changed by users changes depending on `--container-prefix`, `--pod-prefix`, `--separator` and `--name` flags.

Thus it is required to generate the units for a pod with a single command so we know how to setup the dependencies.

> If you generate the units for a pod you should runt he command on the pod and never on individual containers.
> 
> Just practically speaking it is completely impossible to do this because we cannot know the pod unit name. The unit name can be changed by users changes depending on `--container-prefix`, `--pod-prefix`, `--separator` and `--name` flags.
> 
> Thus it is required to generate the units for a pod with a single command so we know how to setup the dependencies.

@Luap99 Yeah, understand it's not expected behavior.
But technically speaking, you don't put a pod name in systemd file, but its ID there. And you always know pod ID, it's in inspection info of container. So you definitely know always what to write.

If a user generated the service with `podman generate systemd --name` the unit name would be `pod-<NAME>.service` not the default `pod-<ID>.service` same for the other mentioned options, thus it is impossible to know the real unit name which we would need to add to `After=`.

For the ansible issue I think the correct thing is to only generate it for the pod. I understand that this is not really obvious
